statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
Santa realized that hand-drawing circles takes too long, so he has turned to magic to meet his production quotas.

There are three types of presents with distinct values $ a $ , $ b $ , and $ c $ . Initially, Santa has exactly one present of each type.

You are given two integers $ m $ and $ k $ , representing Santa's favorite number and the cost of duplication, respectively. Santa can cast the following two types of spells any number of times (possibly zero):

1. Create — Choose a type of present and create one additional present of that type. This spell costs $ x $ mana, where $ x\in \{a,b,c\} $ is the value of the chosen type.
2. Duplicate — Choose a type of present and duplicate all presents of that type. This spell costs $ k $ mana.

Santa wants to perform a sequence of spells such that the sum of the values of all presents is a multiple of $ m $ after the spells.

Determine the minimum amount of mana Santa needs to achieve this. It can be shown that, under the given constraints, such a sequence of spells always exists.

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

The only line of each test case contains five integers $ a $ , $ b $ , $ c $ , $ m $ , and $ k $ ( $ 1\leq a< b< c< m\leq 5\cdot 10^5 $ , $ 1\leq k\leq 5\cdot 10^5 $ ).

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ m $ over all test cases does not exceed $ 5\cdot 10^5 $ .

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ k $ over all test cases does not exceed $ 5\cdot 10^5 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, output a single integer — the minimum amount of mana Santa needs to make the sum of the values of all presents a multiple of $ m $ .

Examples
Sample 1
Input
7
1 2 3 21 4
3 4 5 12 34
3 12 14 18 1
6 7 8 10 3
100 103 282 488 221
307 2000 5096 12018 5764
194093 292793 395323 475619 490151
Output
10
0
17
9
1227
35116
7050242

Note
Let $ \ell $ be the list of values of presents and $ \operatorname{sum}(\ell) $ be the sum of all elements in $ \ell $ .

In the first test case, below is an optimal sequence of operations:

\#OperationValue $ \ell $ after operation $ \operatorname{sum}(\ell) $ Cost0—— $ [1, 2, 3] $ $ 6 $ —1Create $ 3 $ $ [1, 2, 3, 3] $ $ 9 $ $ 3 $ 2Create $ 3 $ $ [1, 2, 3, 3, 3] $ $ 12 $ $ 3 $ 3Duplicate $ 3 $ $ [1, 2, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3, 3] $ $ 21 $ $ 4 $ $ 21=7\cdot 3 $ Total: $ 10 $ In the second test case, it is optimal to not perform any operations because $ 3+4+5=12 $ is already a multiple of $ 12 $ .

In the third test case, below is an optimal sequence of operations:

\#OperationValue $ \ell $ after operation $ \operatorname{sum}(\ell) $ Cost0—— $ [3, 12, 14] $ $ 29 $ —1Duplicate $ 12 $ $ [3, 12, 12, 14] $ $ 41 $ $ 1 $ 2Duplicate $ 14 $ $ [3, 12, 12, 14, 14] $ $ 55 $ $ 1 $ 3Create $ 14 $ $ [3, 12, 12, 14, 14, 14] $ $ 69 $ $ 14 $ 4Duplicate $ 3 $ $ [3, 3, 12, 12, 14, 14, 14] $ $ 72 $ $ 1 $ $ 72=18\cdot 4 $ Total: $ 17 $

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: The operation is clearly independent for each present. Solve the problem for a single type of present with value $$$a$$$.

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.content:
There is one big idea that you need from here, the following small hints may help in getting you there.
Hint 2.1
It may be easier to start with no presents (but eventually force yourself to have all three).
Hint 2.2
Although we considered the operations for a given type of present independently from other types in Hint 1, we are allowed to intertwine operations as we wish.
Hint 2.3
If we tried to merge all three presents into a single graph, the primary issue is that we need to maintain three separate modulos to handle the duplicate spell. Is there any way we can fix this?

hints.title: Hint 2.1
hints.content: It may be easier to start with no presents (but eventually force yourself to have all three).

hints.title: Hint 2.2
hints.content: Although we considered the operations for a given type of present independently from other types in Hint 1, we are allowed to intertwine operations as we wish.

hints.title: Hint 2.3
hints.content: If we tried to merge all three presents into a single graph, the primary issue is that we need to maintain three separate modulos to handle the duplicate spell. Is there any way we can fix this?

solutions.title: Solution
solutions.content:
2178H - Create or Duplicate
The operations are independent for each present so we'll consider them separately. First, we will solve the easier version of only one present type with value $$$a$$$.
When applying an operation, we only care about the current residue of the total sum modulo $$$m$$$. This motivates us to have some DP with this residue as the state. Let $$$dp[x]$$$ denote the minimum cost of a sequence of operations to get to $$$x\text{ mod }m$$$. We have $$$dp[a]=0$$$ and the transitions
$$$dp[x+a\text{ mod } m]=\min(dp[x + a\text{ mod } m], dp[x] + a)$$$ for the Create spell
$$$dp[x\cdot 2\text{ mod } m]=\min(dp[x \cdot 2\text{ mod } m], dp[x] + k)$$$ for the Duplicate spell
Because there is no nice way to iterate over $$$x$$$ in computing these transitions, we must consider a weighted graph representation (states as vertices and transitions as edges). In this graph representation, we can use Dijkstra's algorithm to find the shortest paths from vertex $$$0$$$ which give us the desired $$$dp$$$ values.
We could try to combine the results from above for $$$a$$$, $$$b$$$, and $$$c$$$. However, this would require a $$$(\min, +)$$$ convolution without a nice structure, which will take $$$O(n^2)$$$.
The issue that prevents us from making a graph representation that combines $$$a$$$, $$$b$$$, and $$$c$$$ is that we must maintain separate residues for each value in order to perform updates for the Duplicate spell. For instance, consider the following sequence of spells
Step
1
2

$$$k$$$
$$$c$$$
$$$a$$$
$$$k$$$
$$$k$$$
$$$k$$$
$$$k$$$
$$$c$$$
If we only maintain a single combined residue, we do not know how to update our total value when duplicating a single present. To fix this, instead of considering the operations independently, we will perform them together. For instance, consider the following modification of the same sequence
Step
1
2
3
4
5
6
$$$a$$$
Duplicate
Create
Duplicate
$$$b$$$
Duplicate
Duplicate
Duplicate
$$$c$$$
Create
Duplicate
Create
cost
$$$k$$$
$$$2k$$$
$$$a$$$
$$$c$$$
$$$3k$$$
$$$c$$$
This almost works, where we can duplicate presents together and simply double the combined residue. However, because we started with one of each present, we would be duplicating presents that we do not wish to. To fix this, we can just start with no presents and eventually force ourselves to create one of each present (and subtract $$$a+b+c$$$ from the cost at the end).
Step
1
2

Thus, consider the graph with vertices $$$(\text{current residue}, \text{mask of presents we have at least one of})$$$. Then, we can create the following edges
$$$(x, \text{mask}) \to (x + a\text{ mod } m, \text{mask }|\ 100)$$$ with cost $$$a$$$
$$$(x, \text{mask}) \to (x + b\text{ mod } m, \text{mask } |\ 010)$$$ with cost $$$b$$$
$$$(x, \text{mask}) \to (x + c\text{ mod } m, \text{mask } |\ 001)$$$ with cost $$$c$$$
$$$(x, \text{mask}) \to (x \cdot 2\text{ mod } m, \text{mask})$$$ with cost $$$k\cdot \text{popcount}(\text{mask})$$$
The desired answer is the shortest path from $$$(0, 000)$$$ to $$$(0, 111)$$$, subtracted by $$$a+b+c$$$. This can be found using Dijkstra's algorithm in $$$O(8m\log m)$$$.
